section_text: Awareness of the game horizon does not meaningfully erode this player’s cooperation. Across the late portion of the game, including the final rounds, they continue to contribute the maximum amount. There is no visible “end-game unraveling” where they reduce contributions as the remaining number of rounds shrinks. Instead, their cooperative stance is maintained right through the final decision. They do not attempt to exploit the group in the final round by dropping their contribution, even though doing so would be individually profitable and cannot be punished or rewarded in future interactions. This suggests that their strategy is not based on backward induction about future payoffs, but on a more time-invariant rule or internal standard (“I contribute fully in this kind of game”). Their behavior is consistent with a player who either does not prioritize horizon-based optimization or who values maintaining a cooperative identity or principle more than capturing the one-shot gain from late defection. The absence of contribution decay near the end of the game also indicates that they either do not expect others to massively defect in the final rounds, or, if they do, they still choose to cooperate despite that expectation. In either case, the looming end does not shift them into a more self-interested mode. They remain a stable, high contributor from start to finish, with no systematic end-game adjustment beyond ordinary payoff fluctuations caused by others’ changing behavior.

Despite full knowledge of the finite horizon, this player does not display a classic end-game decay in cooperation. They sustain maximal contributions through the final phase, including the last few rounds and the very last decision, with no observable reduction. There is no evidence that remaining-round information changes their strategy: they do not ramp up free-riding near the end, nor do they escalate contributions (they are already at ceiling most of the time). Awareness of the game’s approaching end therefore either does not factor into their decision rule, or is consciously overridden by a stable norm to keep contributing fully. In the final rounds, even when others’ contributions are mixed or relatively low, they continue to contribute the maximum. This suggests they do not attempt to “cash out” at the end by exploiting the group once future reciprocity is impossible. Instead, their behavior is time-invariant: they cooperate in early, middle, and late stages in essentially the same way. Overall, their horizon awareness appears behaviorally irrelevant; they act like a rule-following, norm-driven cooperator who does not strategically adjust for the end-game.

The player shows clear awareness of the finite horizon and uses the late game to experiment and occasionally exploit, rather than following a simple end-game collapse. Near the final stretch, they continue to alternate between mid-level contributions, free-riding, and full contributions, indicating that they do not adopt a straightforward “contribute nothing at the end” policy. They do not exhibit a smooth decay in cooperation as the game approaches its conclusion. Instead, their behavior remains flexible and context-sensitive: sometimes they reduce contributions sharply even with rounds remaining, and sometimes they sustain high contributions very late. This suggests they do not rigidly apply backward induction logic, but they understand that late-round moves have fewer future repercussions and are therefore more willing to shift to opportunistic strategies. In the very last part of the game, they continue to condition their decisions on what they see others doing, rather than purely on the fact that the game is ending. They are still prepared to fully cooperate in the final round when the group looks sufficiently cooperative, implying that they value the immediate gains from one more cooperative round more than the short-term benefit of a final free-ride in an already cooperative environment. Their end-game approach is thus characterized by: (1) sustained responsiveness to the observed cooperative climate, (2) willingness to occasionally free-ride when it seems low-risk to reputation or future payoffs (even late), and (3) no strong evidence of a strict “last-round defection” principle. This end-game pattern is consistent with a player who understands the horizon but remains primarily driven by short-run payoff calculus and conditional reciprocity rather than by a fully rationalized backward-induction strategy.

The player is aware of the finite horizon and shows a classic end-game shift toward non-cooperation. In the final stretch, they increasingly default to zero contributions, even while others commonly continue to contribute positively. This pattern indicates that as the number of remaining rounds shrinks, they become less willing to “invest” in the public fund and more inclined to secure private coins. They do not attempt any terminal “gift” or last-round cooperative gesture. Instead, the final decision is to free-ride entirely, which is consistent with standard backward-induction reasoning: with no future rounds, there is no strategic value in maintaining a cooperative reputation. This supports the interpretation that their earlier cooperative moves are at least partly strategic and conditional on ongoing interaction, rather than purely driven by unconditional altruism. The end-game response suggests the player treats cooperation as contingent on future benefits and continuation of the relationship. As continuation value approaches zero, their willingness to support the group decays, and they rely on others’ residual cooperation to maintain high payoffs for themselves.

The player shows awareness of the finite horizon but does not strongly unravel their cooperation near the end. In the final phase of the game they continue to make substantial contributions, sometimes at the maximum level and sometimes at a medium–high level, without any clear downward trend toward full defection. There is no evidence of a systematic end-game collapse in their own strategy. Instead of gradually decreasing contributions as the last rounds approach, they maintain their established pattern of conditional generosity with some variation. Their behavior suggests that they either discount the classical backward-induction logic, prioritize maintaining a cooperative norm for its own sake, or simply continue to apply the same myopic, round-by-round conditional rule without special adjustment for the fact that the game is ending. The absence of a sharp final-round free-ride, and the presence of a high final contribution, implies that they do not treat the end-game as a unique opportunity to exploit the group. Their horizon awareness is observable in the sense that they know the number of rounds, but their decisions indicate either limited strategic use of that information or a preference structure in which sustaining cooperation remains valuable even when there is no future interaction in this specific game.

This player shows no classic end-game unraveling. Contributions remain at their established baseline through the final phase, including the very last round, with no visible decay, escalation, or switching to exploitation even when they know the game is about to end. They do not ratchet contributions up to be altruistic “on the way out,” nor do they drop to free-ride in the final opportunities. The lack of horizon effects suggests they either (a) are not strategically focused on the finite horizon, (b) prioritize following their internal contribution rule over short-run optimization, or (c) do not strongly believe that others will change behavior at the end and therefore see no reason to adjust. In new PGG environments, this indicates that their cooperation level is likely governed more by a stable personal policy than by forward-looking end-game logic, and that they maintain that policy even when continuation incentives disappear.

The player shows no end-game defection or decay in cooperation. Their contribution level remains at the maximum until and including the final round, despite having full knowledge of the round horizon. They do not exhibit the commonly observed pattern of gradually lowering contributions as the game approaches its conclusion, nor do they react to others’ variable behavior near the end. This suggests that their strategy is not anchored in backward induction or finite-horizon reasoning, but in a time-invariant norm: “I contribute fully every round,” irrespective of how many rounds remain. There is no evidence of last-round opportunism, payoff grabbing, or even subtle shading down of contributions. Their end-game behavior reinforces the view that they are a principled, norm-following cooperator whose choices do not hinge on future interaction or on maintaining reputational incentives within the game.

The player appears aware of the finite horizon but does not strongly unravel their cooperation near the end. Their contributions late in the game remain substantial: they do not shift to a purely self‑interested, low‑contribution strategy as the last round approaches. There is some adjustment in the final phase, with contributions becoming more moderate rather than maximal, but the player still contributes a meaningful share of their endowment in the last round. This suggests they either discount the classic backward‑induction logic or give weight to intrinsic or fairness motives that persist even when future retaliation or reward is impossible. There is no evidence of a strategic “last‑round grab”; instead, their end‑game response looks like a cautious reduction in generosity under uncertainty, not a collapse of cooperation. Overall, end‑game awareness is present but does not dominate their cooperative disposition.

They remain fully cooperative to the final round and do not reduce contributions as the horizon approaches. End-game awareness appears mainly in their punishment planning, not in their contribution strategy. Near the end, they explicitly reference the remaining round(s) to coordinate a last, large-scale punitive attack aimed at driving a target’s payoff toward zero, asking others to “sacrifice” their own payoff to achieve this. This shows that, as the game nears its conclusion, they escalate antisocial punishment of disliked players rather than relaxing enforcement or shifting to personal profit maximization. They do not show any attempt to conserve resources or reduce punishment late in the game to maximize earnings; instead, they treat the looming end as an opportunity to finish the norm-enforcement and moral “lesson” against targets. There is no evidence of strategic cooperation decay; contribution stays maxed and punishment intensity against disliked individuals either persists or intensifies.

Awareness of the finite horizon is present at least implicitly, as the group references remaining rounds and contemplates a final‑round punishment coordination. This player does not reduce contributions near the end; they maintain max contributions through the final round and do not pivot to self‑interested free‑riding. Their punishment behavior continues late into the game and is even considered for a coordinated final “hit” on a target, suggesting that the end game is seen as an opportunity to complete a punitive campaign rather than to harvest private gains. They show willingness to sacrifice late‑game earnings to damage a disliked player, but also occasionally forgo punishment (including in the final round), possibly to conserve payoff or because prior punishment is perceived as sufficient. There is no visible attempt to exploit the final rounds for unilateral gain; instead, they stay aligned with the cooperative‑and‑punitive norm. Strategic sophistication about backward induction is not evident; their behavior reflects norm loyalty and group‑based hostility more than horizon‑driven recalculation.

They show clear awareness of the game horizon but do not drastically change their contribution behavior at the end. Contributions in the final phase remain within the same low-to-mid band as earlier, without any sharp collapse or spike in cooperation. This indicates that they treat the end game as a continuation of their established strategy rather than an opportunity to either fully defect or demonstrate late altruism. Where they do show an end-game shift is in their use of punishment: as the game approaches its conclusion, they are willing to invest more heavily in sanctioning. Since there is no future interaction to recoup these costs, this late escalation implies that punishment is not purely instrumental for future-payoff incentives; they likely have a preference for norm enforcement or for expressing disapproval, even when there is little scope for future deterrence. In sum, their end-game pattern is: maintain established contribution levels, but loosen payoff-maximizing constraints on punishment, suggesting stable cooperative caution but stronger willingness to “settle accounts” as the interaction ends.

The player shows awareness of the finite horizon but does not engage in a drastic end‑game collapse of cooperation. Near the end of the game, contributions fluctuate but remain generally high or moderately high rather than dropping toward zero. Even in the final round, they are willing to contribute the entire endowment despite substantial variation and some very low contributions from others. Instead of a linear decay, their late‑game behavior reflects an interaction between short‑term incentives and experienced group behavior. After periods where others reduce contributions or where the pattern of cooperation is mixed, they sometimes cut their own contribution back to an intermediate level. However, they also show readiness to restore full contributions in later rounds, including the last round, if they still perceive enough cooperative potential. This pattern suggests they do not fully switch to backward‑induction logic that would predict minimal contributions at the horizon. They appear to maintain a cooperation norm independent of pure final‑round payoff optimization. Their strategy in the end game continues to mix principled cooperation with modest self‑protection, rather than fully exploiting the finite horizon.

The player displays horizon awareness but does not collapse into pure defection at the end. In late rounds, contributions do not monotonically decline; instead, they remain in a moderate band and sometimes increase relative to nearby rounds. This indicates that the player continues to care about the public good and/or maintaining a fairness standard, even when there is little or no future opportunity for reciprocity or learning. At the same time, end-game behavior is not naively cooperative. The player simultaneously increases their use of costly punishment late in the game, which suggests an understanding that direct payoff from shaping others’ behavior is limited at this stage, and that end-game punishment functions more as final norm expression than as an investment in future cooperation. Contribution decisions in these final periods seem to balance three motives: preserving some private payoff, maintaining a self-image or norm of not fully free-riding, and backing up that norm through strong enforcement. There is no strong evidence that they are trying to curry favor or avoid retaliation, since punishers and victims are anonymous and future interactions are minimal; thus any sustained cooperation near the end is likely internally motivated rather than purely strategic. In new environments with visible endpoints, this player is likely to keep contributing at mid-range levels rather than sharply defecting, while potentially intensifying punitive actions as the game nears its conclusion, using the final rounds to underline fairness expectations rather than to maximize last-period income.

Awareness of the finite horizon does not produce a simple monotonic decay in cooperation. Instead, the player continues their pattern of alternating between mid-range contributions and very low contributions through the later part of the game. Even very late, they sometimes cooperate at moderate levels and sometimes free-ride, rather than converging on full defection. Severe punishment episodes near the end are followed immediately by higher, more cooperative contributions, indicating that direct payoff feedback matters more to them than abstract end-game logic. They do not appear to adopt a final-round “grab everything” policy; they are still willing to contribute almost nothing in some late rounds but also still return to medium contributions after that. This suggests that, although they can see how many rounds remain, their horizon awareness is weaker than their short-run response to sanctions. In summary, their end-game behavior is best described as continuing sanction-driven conditional cooperation with opportunistic dips, rather than a deliberate backward-induction collapse of contributions.

The player is aware of the finite horizon and shows classic end-game defection behavior. As the game approaches its conclusion, they move from willingness to contribute significantly above zero to sharp reductions and finally to complete non-contribution. They do not attempt a last-round cooperative gesture and instead prioritize personal payoff once the opportunity for sustained reciprocal cooperation effectively vanishes. Their late-game choices indicate that long-run reputation or future punishment are no longer salient enough to justify costly cooperation; they switch emphasis from group efficiency to individual gain. Exposure to heavy punishment near the end coincides with a sharp drop in their giving, suggesting that negative social feedback at this stage reinforces the logic of “take what you can before it’s over” rather than nudging them back toward cooperation. Overall, in terminal periods they behave as a payoff-focused, distrustful player who no longer expects meaningful cooperative returns from the group.

The player shows clear awareness of the game horizon and does not follow the classic end-game decay predicted by backward induction. Instead, they escalate their cooperation at the very end, culminating in fully cooperative choices in the final stages. This indicates that they do not treat the last rounds as an opportunity to “cash out” via maximum free-riding. This terminal escalation suggests one or more of the following, all with low to moderate confidence: they may hold a norm of “finishing well,” they may derive intrinsic satisfaction from cooperating even when instrumental incentives are weaker, or they may hope that others sustain or increase cooperation despite the approaching end. There is no observable effort to exploit the final round by minimizing contributions, so strategic last-round defection is not part of their behavioral repertoire here. Their choices also indicate that negative experiences (such as being punished earlier in the game) do not push them into a retaliatory end-game spiral of defection. Instead, they maintain or raise their cooperative stance as the game closes. They appear more inclined to define their end-game identity around contributing rather than around maximizing short-run earnings. Overall, the end-game response suggests a cooperative self-concept that is somewhat robust to standard deterioration incentives. They are willing to leave money on the table in the final round in order to act in line with that self-concept or with a perceived social norm of contributing, even without expectation of future reciprocation.

They show limited but noticeable end-game adjustment. Late in the game, when the horizon is relatively close and group behavior becomes less uniform, they temporarily reduce their contributions while still remaining cooperative relative to others. However, they do not follow through with a full end-game unraveling; they subsequently revert to maximum contributions even when the game is nearly over. This suggests some awareness that the environment is changing as the end approaches, but their underlying cooperative norm remains dominant over pure backward-induction logic. They do not appear to adopt a “grab what you can at the end” mindset; instead, they briefly test lower contributions under uncertainty and then recommit to high cooperation, implying a preference for consistency and group welfare even near the final round.

They show no visible end-game defection. Even when other players reduce contributions late in the game and they are materially exploited, they continue to contribute fully both immediately afterward and in the final round. The looming end of the game, visible in the interface, does not appear to change their strategy or induce decay in cooperation. They do not shift toward self-protection or opportunism as the horizon shortens, suggesting either a principled or habit-driven cooperative rule that overrides standard backward-induction reasoning. Any subtle change in beliefs about others near the end is unknown; behaviorally, they remain fully cooperative.

They show limited and somewhat muted end-game defection. As the horizon approaches, when new or previously inactive players reappear and contribute less, they modestly reduce their own contribution while still contributing more than the new group average and securing a high payoff. This indicates awareness of the changing environment and possible end-game incentives, but they do not embrace a full unraveling of cooperation. In the final round, once the group returns to full contributions, they immediately revert to full cooperation, even though there is no future to influence. That pattern suggests that their contributions are partly driven by a rule-based or identity-based cooperative norm (“we contribute max when others do”) rather than pure backward induction. Overall, end-game effects are present but weak: they fine-tune rather than abandon cooperation.

They show no end-game decay in cooperation. Even when others reduce contributions near the end and when there is clear horizon information, they continue to contribute fully. After a late deviation where they do use punishment, they immediately return to full cooperation in the final round, not shifting to self-protection or revenge. They do not appear to adopt the usual backward-induction logic; they keep behaving as if the cooperative norm should hold until the very last round. Their horizon awareness in terms of payoff optimization is unknown; behavior indicates either disregard for end-game incentives or a strong commitment to a stable norm that overrides such considerations.

They show no visible end-game defection or decay in cooperation. Their contributions remain at or near maximum through the final rounds, unaffected by the shrinking horizon. There is a tiny, isolated deviation from full contribution late in the game, but they immediately revert to full giving and do not move toward self-serving behavior as the final round approaches. They do not appear to leverage the knowledge of how many rounds remain in a strategic way. The absence of any ramp-down—even after repeated evidence that many group members are not reliably reciprocating—suggests either that they prioritize a stable cooperative identity over last-round exploitation, or that they are not modelling the strategic implications of the end of the game in a payoff-maximizing way. In practice, their “type” is time-invariant: early-, mid-, and late-game behavior look essentially the same.

The player shows only a weak and inconsistent end-game effect. They do not systematically ramp up to maximal free-riding at the end, nor do they mount a final cooperative push. Instead, their late behavior continues the earlier opportunistic mix: some zero contributions and some small positive ones, with one late larger contribution interspersed among freeriding choices. They clearly see that the game has a finite horizon, but they do not exploit the final rounds with an extreme shift in either direction. This suggests that for them, the horizon information is not the main driver; rather, they maintain their general pattern: contribute when it feels situationally appropriate but otherwise free-ride. Any end-game decay in cooperation is modest and partially masked by their pre-existing tendency to contribute less than others.

They show clear awareness of the finite horizon and act accordingly. Near the end of the game they revert decisively to zero contributions in alternating fashion, including in the final round, even while several others still contribute high amounts. There is no sign of a “last-round generosity” impulse; instead, they end by exploiting the remaining cooperators. This pattern indicates that, as the end approaches, they increasingly prioritize short-term private payoff over sustaining group welfare and do not expect future reciprocal benefits. Their cooperation in late rounds is intermittent and quickly gives way to freeriding once the end of the game looms large, consistent with standard end-game defection logic.

There is no detectable end‑game decay in cooperation: their contribution behavior is identical from start to finish, including the final rounds. They show no visible horizon‑driven shift toward free‑riding, even after being punished. Their use of punishment also persists into the last rounds against persistent defectors, indicating they do not reduce sanctioning effort merely because the game is about to end. They appear aware that payoffs convert to real money and refer to the bonus structure, but this awareness does not lead to strategic tapering of contributions or avoidance of costly punishment near the end. Overall, they behave as if the cooperative norm and enforcement principle are more important than exploiting the finite horizon.

There is little evidence of a classic “end-game collapse” pattern. The player’s contribution profile near the end stays mostly aligned with their general cooperative tendency. They do not make a clear, strategic shift to sustained free‑riding in the final rounds despite knowing the horizon length, nor do they ramp up contributions uniquely at the very end. Their occasional switch to non‑contribution occurs in the later middle of the game, but it is brief and reversed quickly. After testing defection and encountering strong punishment, they largely return to full contributions, including in the last stretch. This suggests that horizon awareness does not dominate their behavior; they appear more anchored in ongoing group norms and recent social feedback than in backward induction logic. Any end‑game awareness influence is weak or unknown.

There is no clear evidence of a strong end-game unraveling in their contributions. They continue to choose the maximum contribution in most of the later rounds and do not show a monotonic decline as the game approaches its final stages. Instances of zero contribution later in the game coincide with broader breakdowns in group cooperation, not specifically with the known horizon. They appear aware of the structure (rounds are numbered and discussed by others), but their behavior does not show a systematic “it’s almost over, so I defect” strategy. Instead, their late-game choices oscillate with group behavior: they defect in one late round during a wider collapse in contributions and return to full contribution once cooperation resumes. Their final round behavior remains cooperative even though the incentive to defect is highest, suggesting either a stable internal norm for contributing or continued desire to align with the cooperative majority more than a horizon-focused payoff maximization. Given the limited direct evidence, their horizon awareness is unknown, but their behavior is more consistent with norm-following than with calculated end-game exploitation.

Awareness of the finite horizon does not erode their cooperation. They maintain identical, maximal contributions through the entire game, including the final rounds. There is no visible end‑game unraveling on their part; they do not attempt last‑minute profit‑taking or strategic defection. The only strategic shift near the end is the introduction of one targeted punishment when cooperation visibly drops for a subset of players, but this does not translate into any reduction in their own contributions. Their horizon awareness seems to manifest, at most, in a modest willingness to sanction near the end, not in any willingness to slacken their own cooperative effort. Overall, they behave as if norms or principles override backward‑induction logic.

The player shows limited but noticeable horizon awareness. For most of the game, they behave as if cooperation is to be maintained regardless of time remaining. Near the end, they continue to fully cooperate even when others reduce their contributions, and they still spend on rewarding high contributors shortly before the final round. Only in the very last decision do they slightly reduce their own contribution instead of cleanly switching to defection. This suggests they recognize the approaching end but do not abandon their cooperative identity or shift into an aggressively self-interested mode. There is no systematic decay of contributions; instead, they preserve the cooperative norm and only marginally relax it at the final opportunity, implying that maintaining a cooperative pattern matters to them even when future reciprocation is no longer possible.

The player is clearly horizon‑aware: when the number of remaining rounds becomes very small and others begin to lower contributions, they finally reduce their own contribution as well. Up to that point, they maintain maximum cooperation despite noise and free‑riding, but the combination of impending termination and visible contribution declines appears to relax their commitment to full cooperation. They do not escalate punishment or reward activity in the final round and instead remain passive in the social enforcement stage. This suggests they do not treat the end game as an opportunity for last‑minute retribution or reciprocal thanks. Instead, they adjust their direct contribution to align more closely with the emerging, slightly lower group norm and with the reduced future benefits of maintaining a strictly cooperative stance. Overall, end‑game behavior shows a modest decay in cooperation, but not a collapse: they still contribute a positive, substantial amount and do not switch to pure defection. This points to a type that recognizes the strategic logic of end games but is not purely driven by backward induction.

This player shows little evidence of end-game unraveling. After switching back to full contribution, they maintain high contributions through the final rounds, even as the horizon approaches and others begin to lower their contributions. They do not appear to exploit the final periods by cutting back, despite clear knowledge of the remaining number of rounds. This pattern suggests either limited strategic anticipation of end-game dynamics, or a preference for stable cooperative behavior over last-moment opportunism. They may treat “cooperate once stable” as a default rule that they follow independent of the time horizon. Whether this reflects principled cooperation, inertia, or a belief that others may still punish in late rounds is unknown.

They exhibit no end-game decline in cooperation. Even as the horizon shortens and others start reducing their contributions, they maintain full giving to the public good. This indicates clear horizon awareness (the remaining-rounds information is present) but no opportunistic shift: they do not try to exploit the final rounds, nor do they “give up” on cooperation when others become less cooperative near the end. Their strategy is time-invariant, suggesting either a principle-based or rule-based approach to cooperation that does not depend on future interaction value.

The player shows no observable end-game defection. Their contributions stay at the maximum until the very last round, despite the clear information about the finite horizon and observable decreases in some others’ contributions near the end. They do not appear to adjust their strategy in anticipation of the game concluding; neither a decay in cooperation nor a last-round grab for personal payoff is evident. There is also no compensatory use of punishment or reward at the end to “correct” late-stage free-riding. Overall, their pattern fits an agent who follows a stable cooperation rule independent of horizon length, with no visible end-game opportunism or strategic winding down.

As the game approaches its conclusion, their contribution level declines modestly, not sharply. They do not “unravel” to zero; instead, they cut their own giving while most others remain cooperative. This pattern indicates clear horizon awareness: they recognize the diminishing future value of maintaining full cooperation and start to secure slightly higher private payoffs. However, they still remain contributors rather than defectors, suggesting that their cooperative disposition partially constrains pure end-game profit-taking. Simultaneously, they ramp up their use of rewards right at the very end, directing resources toward others despite the absence of future rounds in which such incentives could change behavior. That last-moment generosity points to a motivation beyond strict forward-looking strategy—possibly a preference for expressing approval or “thanking” perceived cooperators—coexisting with a mild end-game self-favoring cut in contributions.

They do not significantly reduce their contribution level as the game approaches the final rounds; they continue to choose the cooperative contribution option even in the last round. However, their use of punishment becomes more targeted at the very end, extending sanctions to a persistent non-contributor who had been free-riding across many rounds. This suggests some awareness that consequences can be imposed even when there is no future to shape behavior, implying a mixture of norm-enforcement and perhaps expressive or retributive motives rather than purely forward-looking deterrence. There is no clear evidence that they change their cooperative strategy purely because the game is ending; instead, end-game effects appear primarily in the choice of whom to punish, not whether to contribute.

They show no visible end-game effect. Contributions remain maximal through to the very last round, with no reduction when the horizon is near and no strategic shift in punishment or reward behavior at the end. They neither exploit the last rounds for personal gain nor relax their cooperative standard when mutual future enforcement is less relevant. This pattern suggests that their cooperation is principle-driven rather than purely strategic with respect to the game’s finite horizon. Their use of punishment does not intensify or taper specifically around the final rounds; instead, they return to not using punishment at the very end, consistent with their generally sparse use of sanctions.

They show no end‑game decay in cooperation. They continue to contribute the maximum amount until the final round and maintain the same general pattern of selective punishment. They do not appear to shift toward short-term payoff maximization as the remaining rounds shrink, nor do they dramatically ramp up punishment or reward in anticipation of the game ending. Horizon awareness is unknown from their own words, but their unchanged behavior across the game suggests either indifference to the horizon or a principle-driven strategy that is not adjusted by the finite length.

This player displays clear horizon awareness but does not use it to unravel cooperation. Their contribution stays high through the final round, including after they have already observed extensive free-riding and intense punishment dynamics. Their one defection occurs close to the end but is not followed by further defection; they return to full cooperation immediately afterward, even though there is no future round in which others could reciprocate. That pattern is inconsistent with a purely backward-induction, payoff-maximizing end-game strategy. In contrast, they escalate or at least maintain strong punishment use in the last round, targeting chronic free-riders again despite the absence of any future opportunity to influence behavior. This suggests that their late-game actions are driven more by retributive or norm-expressive motives—wanting defectors to “feel the burn”—than by strategic deterrence. The end game becomes an opportunity to reaffirm norms and express disapproval rather than to cash out selfishly. Thus, when faced with the game’s endpoint, they prioritize moral or expressive goals (punishing violators while still contributing) over maximizing their own final payoff.

They clearly recognize the finite horizon and adjust behavior in the final phase. Despite being a habitual free-rider, they choose to fully contribute in the last round, when there is no future opportunity for others to reciprocate or retaliate. This indicates that end-game behavior is not about long-term deterrence or relationship-building; rather, it suggests a desire to align with the apparent group pattern at the close, perhaps as a form of conformity, image maintenance, or simple “finish on a cooperative note” preference. Their contributions do not steadily decay or escalate toward the end; instead, their pattern remains inconsistent, with free-riding continuing late and only a final-round cooperative gesture. This implies that they do not follow the classic backward-induction logic of unraveling cooperation, nor do they show a strong, principled commitment to maintain high cooperation all the way through. The final cooperative move appears symbolic or impressionistic rather than part of a calculated long-term reciprocal strategy. <PU﻿NISHMENT> They never use the punishment mechanism at any point, even when there are clear and repeated free-riders and even when other players strongly call for punishing specific targets. Their non-use persists despite strong social cues encouraging punishment and despite themselves being a major punishment target later on. This consistent abstention suggests that they either (a) judge punishment as not worth the cost in terms of payoff, (b) dislike the role of enforcer or moral arbiter, or (c) prefer to free-ride not only on contributions but also on others’ enforcement efforts. Given their payoff-maximizing behavior in contributions, the most parsimonious interpretation is that they see punishment as an inefficient investment that reduces their earnings, even if it might improve group behavior. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives: despite being heavily punished, they never punish back. That could reflect emotional detachment, a pragmatic refusal to escalate costly conflict, or a sense that the punishing coalition is too large to challenge. They do not selectively punish low contributors, so there is no sign they seek to shape others’ contributions through sanctions. They are best described as a “non-enforcer,” content to let others bear the enforcement costs while adapting their own strategy largely independently.

They show clear awareness of the game horizon; they refer explicitly to the possibility that the current situation is the end and offer closing remarks about everyone’s performance. Despite this awareness, there is no end-game unraveling in their behavior: they maintain full contributions through the final rounds and do not reduce cooperation as the end approaches. They also do not switch into opportunistic or retaliatory use of punishment at the end, even when the social norm of “get the free rider” is well entrenched and others are spending aggressively. Instead, they preserve the same cooperative pattern and close with a light normative reminder rather than strategic exploitation or last-minute revenge. This suggests they are more rule- or principle-driven than horizon-driven; the impending end of interaction does not meaningfully alter their decisions.

They show clear awareness of the finite horizon but do not simply follow classic end-game unraveling. Near the end, they briefly defect by keeping their entire endowment while many others still contribute; this looks like a one-off opportunistic move once payoffs are locked-in soon and reputational concerns beyond the session no longer matter. However, in the very last round they revert to full contribution, suggesting they either still value the cooperative payoff from one more mutual-contribution round or they have a residual norm of “finish cooperatively.” They also choose to punish a known free-rider again in the final round, which indicates that, even in the very last opportunity with no future rounds, they maintain a willingness to pay to sanction norm violators. End-game behavior thus mixes a short, opportunistic defection with persistent norm-enforcement and cooperative identity.

Their strategy shows no visible end-game shift.
